Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies tryptamine for monoterpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression alone produced only unsustained increases in alkaloid accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper; core summarized enzyme in MIA pathway. Localizes to epidermis of aerial organs and apical meristem of roots in Catharanthus roseus.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Abundant in roots and also occurs in photosynthetic organs; epidermis of stems, leaves, and flower buds; cells near root apical meristem
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Abundant in roots and photosynthetic organs
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: EST databases; transcriptomics/metabolomics correlation in C. roseus; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TDC converts tryptophan to tryptamine as an early step in MIA biosynthesis. 2. TDC is localized to epidermal cells of aerial organs and apical root tissues in C. roseus. 3. Constitutive TDC overexpression alone did not durably increase alkaloid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; functional expression; in situ hybridization; immunolocalization; transgenic overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light and methyl jasmonate-responsive alkaloid induction context
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates tryptophan to form tryptamine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against herbivores and pathogens; pharmaceutical alkaloid production
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as part of multigene engineering, but single-gene overexpression is insufficient for strong MIA enhancement

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls entry into secologanin biosynthesis feeding MIA production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also called geraniol 10-hydroxylase (G10H); first committed step in secologanin biosynthesis; localized to internal phloem parenchyma of aerial organs.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Internal phloem-associated parenchyma of young aerial organs
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: EST databases; transcript profiling in C. roseus
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Secologanin branch of monoterpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyp76B6/G10H catalyzes the first committed step of secologanin biosynthesis. 2. It was purified and cloned from C. roseus. 3. Its cell-type localization supports intercellular trafficking of MIA intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein purification; cloning; localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates geraniol at C10 in the secologanin pathway
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target to enhance secologanin supply when stacked with downstream genes/regulators

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to secologanin formation and thus MIA precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Recently discovered from C. roseus EST resources at time of review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LAMT was identified through C. roseus EST resources. 2. It catalyzes methylation of loganic acid in the secologanin pathway. 3. It fills a previously uncharacterized step feeding MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA discovery from EST resource; inferred pathway placement
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates loganic acid to form loganin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for improving precursor flux in secologanin-limited systems

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces secologanin, the monoterpenoid precursor of all MIAs
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also called secologanin synthase (SLS); localizes to epidermis of aerial organs and apical meristem in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Epidermis of stems, leaves, flower buds; cells near root apical meristem
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyp72A1 catalyzes oxidative C-C cleavage of loganin to secologanin. 2. It performs the final reaction of the early secologanin branch. 3. Its epidermal localization indicates multicellular MIA pathway organization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; enzyme assignment; localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively cleaves loganin to generate secologanin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for boosting secologanin supply to increase MIA flux

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Forms strictosidine, the common precursor to all MIAs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive overexpression alone did not produce sustained alkaloid increases
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: First alkaloid biosynthetic cDNA cloned and functionally expressed in microorganisms; orthologs from Rauvolfia serpentina and Ophiorrhiza pumila also summarized.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Roots and photosynthetic organs; epidermis of stems, leaves, flower buds; cells near root apical meristem
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STR catalyzes condensation of tryptamine and secologanin to strictosidine. 2. Strictosidine is the universal precursor of MIAs. 3. Overexpression alone was insufficient for sustained enhancement of alkaloid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; heterologous functional expression; structural analysis; transgenic overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes Pictet-Spengler condensation of tryptamine and secologanin to strictosidine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important stacking partner but not sufficient alone; combine with precursor and regulatory modules

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Removes glucose from strictosidine to release reactive aglycone for branch diversification
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Soluble enzyme associated with cytoplasmic face of endoplasmic reticulum
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SGD from Rauvolfia shows exclusive specificity to strictosidine and close analogs.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Branchpoint-enabling step; not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SGD hydrolyzes strictosidine to its aglycone. 2. This reaction enables formation of branchpoint intermediates leading to diverse MIAs. 3. SGD and RG differ markedly in substrate specificity despite homology.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional characterization; structural analysis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes the glucose moiety of strictosidine to form strictosidine aglycone
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for pathway unlocking but may require coordinated downstream capacity

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates the vindoline-specific branch from tabersonine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves/shoot laticifers and idioblasts for vindoline
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also Cyp71D12; identified from subset of P450 cDNAs induced by light in C. roseus cultures.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Restricted to young leaves and other shoot organs where vindoline biosynthesis occurs
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. T16H catalyzes the first reaction leading from tabersonine to vindoline. 2. The corresponding cDNA was identified among light-induced P450s. 3. It marks the tissue-specific vindoline branch in aerial organs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA identification; functional assignment; localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates tabersonine at C16
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Vindoline contributes to vinblastine formation used as anticancer drug precursor
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key branch-entry target for vindoline/vinblastine engineering in aerial tissues

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes vindoline formation by hydroxylating desacetoxyvindoline
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: ORCA3 activates D4H but alone insufficient without precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Restricted to young leaves and shoot organs; cloned from C. roseus.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Young leaves; shoot organs; laticifers and idioblasts
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. D4H converts desacetoxyvindoline to desacetylvindoline. 2. It is one of the cloned late vindoline-pathway enzymes. 3. It is activated by ORCA3 and localized to laticifers/idioblasts of shoots.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; biochemical characterization; localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates desacetoxyvindoline at position 4
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Vindoline is a precursor of vinblastine
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful late-pathway engineering target in combination with DAT and precursor supply genes

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Completes vindoline biosynthesis, enabling vinblastine formation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not activated by ORCA3; may constrain full pathway activation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BAHD acyltransferase; restricted to young leaves/shoots; accepts only natural substrate unlike MAT.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Young leaves and other shoot organs; laticifers and idioblasts
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Late terminal determinant for vindoline branch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DAT acetylates desacetylvindoline to produce vindoline. 2. It is a BAHD acyltransferase cloned from C. roseus. 3. DAT expression is spatially restricted and not activated by ORCA3, highlighting incomplete regulatory coverage.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; biochemical characterization; localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to desacetylvindoline
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Vindoline contributes to vinblastine anticancer alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Terminal-step engineering target; should be combined with upstream branch genes and transport considerations

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Advances the polyneuridine aldehyde branch toward ajmaline-related alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highly specific esterase from Rauvolfia serpentina; member of alpha/beta hydrolase superfamily.
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Polyneuridine aldehyde branch of MIA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PNAE de-esterifies polyneuridine aldehyde to epi-vellosimine. 2. Recombinant PNAE showed high substrate specificity. 3. Structural work classified PNAE in the alpha/beta hydrolase superfamily.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant enzyme assay; structural modeling; mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes polyneuridine aldehyde ester to produce epi-vellosimine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ajmaline is an antiarrhythmic alkaloid
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for branch-specific ajmaline pathway reconstruction

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Forms vinorine in the ajmaline branch
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BAHD acyltransferase; first BAHD with 3D structure reported.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VS forms vinorine in the ajmaline pathway. 2. It belongs to the BAHD acyltransferase family. 3. Structural analysis identified catalytic importance of the HXXXD motif.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; recombinant enzyme assay; X-ray crystallography; mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates pathway intermediate to form vinorine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ajmaline is used for treatment of arrhythmias
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for microbial or plant reconstruction of ajmaline branch chemistry

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Hydrolyzes acetylajmaline to produce ajmaline
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible parallel pathway through norajmaline suggested
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Active recombinant protein produced via virus expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; belongs to GDSL lipase family.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Virus expression system in Nicotiana benthamiana for recombinant production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAE removes the 17-O-acetyl group from acetylajmaline to form ajmaline. 2. Recombinant AAE showed slight preference for acetylajmaline over norajmaline. 3. The data suggest a possible parallel route with later N-methylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression in N. benthamiana; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes acetylajmaline to ajmaline by removing the acetyl group
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ajmaline is an antiarrhythmic compound
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful terminal-step enzyme for ajmaline pathway completion in heterologous systems

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deglycosylates raucaffricine in Rauvolfia cultures and can also act on strictosidine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Shares ~60% identity with SGD but differs in substrate profile.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RG is a raucaffricine O-beta-glucosidase identified from R. serpentina. 2. It accepts raucaffricine and strictosidine, unlike SGD which does not accept raucaffricine. 3. It illustrates diversification through closely related glucosidases with different specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes the glucose moiety of raucaffricine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May help manipulate glycosylated vs aglycone alkaloid pools in Rauvolfia systems
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/modification of target product

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides tyramine/dopamine precursors for benzylisoquinoline alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Laticifers for BIAs, though transcripts localize to phloem-associated cells
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Large gene family with ~15 members in opium poppy.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcripts localize to phloem, not laticifers
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: EST datasets from opium poppy tissues/cell cultures
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TYDC initiates BIA precursor formation from aromatic amino acids. 2. It is encoded by a large gene family in opium poppy. 3. Its transcript localization to phloem helped revise the cell biology of BIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene family analysis; in situ hybridization
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fungal elicitor induces alkaloid pathway context
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Decarboxylates tyrosine to tyramine or DOPA to dopamine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense; production of morphine, codeine, sanguinarine, berberine and related pharmaceuticals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for precursor enhancement but requires downstream pathway coordination

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes first committed step to (S)-norcoclaurine in BIA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In vivo relative contribution of PR10-like NCS versus alternative dioxygenase-like NCS remains unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Laticifers/other BIA-accumulating tissues depending on species
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also characterized from Thalictrum flavum and Coptis japonica; two different protein families can catalyze the same reaction in vitro.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NCS catalyzes the first committed condensation yielding (S)-norcoclaurine. 2. cDNAs were isolated from opium poppy and T. flavum, and a distinct enzyme form was also found in C. japonica. 3. The review highlights convergent catalytic solutions for this key entry step.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification; cDNA cloning; functional characterization; mechanistic enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PR10/Bet v 1-like protein family; alternative dioxygenase-like form in C. japonica
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses dopamine and 4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde to form (S)-norcoclaurine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutically important BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime entry-point enzyme for BIA pathway reconstruction and flux control

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes BIA accumulation and may be rate-limiting in some Eschscholzia cultures
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression in E. californica cells increased alkaloid content; the line appeared to lack a functional endogenous 6OMT.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as rate-limiting in one cell culture system
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 6OMT catalyzes regiospecific 6-O-methylation in early BIA biosynthesis. 2. Constitutive overexpression in E. californica increased alkaloid levels, unlike 4'OMT. 3. This supports a rate-limiting role in that culture system.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; functional characterization; transgenic overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to the 6-hydroxyl of norcoclaurine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for flux enhancement in early BIA pathway, especially in low-6OMT backgrounds

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes 4'-O-methylation toward (S)-reticuline but had little effect when overexpressed alone in E. californica
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: E. californica 4'OMT also showed low 6OMT activity, suggesting surrogate function.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 4'OMT catalyzes a late methylation step leading to reticuline. 2. Overexpression in E. californica had little effect on alkaloid accumulation. 3. The enzyme may partially substitute for 6OMT in certain cell cultures.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional characterization; transgenic overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates 3'-hydroxy-N-methylcoclaurine at the 4' position
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary engineering target; less impactful alone than 6OMT in tested cells

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides N-methylated intermediate for reticuline formation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: More closely related to cyclopropane fatty acid synthases than to other N-methyltransferases.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein in sieve elements; transcript in associated companion cells
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNMT catalyzes N-methylation of coclaurine in early BIA biosynthesis. 2. It belongs to an unusual methyltransferase family related to cyclopropane fatty acid synthases. 3. In opium poppy, the protein localizes to sieve elements.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; functional characterization; localization
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: SAM-dependent N-methyltransferase; cyclopropane fatty acid synthase-related
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to coclaurine nitrogen to produce N-methylcoclaurine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceutical activity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful component for pathway reconstitution and phloem-targeted engineering

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls an early hydroxylation step affecting overall BIA content
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: ER-associated; also localized to sieve elements/ER contexts
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression or antisense suppression altered total alkaloid content but not profile
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Transgenic modulation changed alkaloid content as expected for an early pathway step.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein localizes to sieve elements in opium poppy; induced in cultured cells with sanguinarine branch context
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Constitutive or antisense suppression in transgenic plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyp80B3 hydroxylates N-methylcoclaurine in an early common BIA step. 2. Transgenic overexpression or antisense suppression substantially changed alkaloid amount but not composition. 3. Localization studies linked it to ER/sieve element-associated metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional characterization; transgenic overexpression/antisense; localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates N-methylcoclaurine at the 3' position
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense and pharmaceuticals such as morphine and sanguinarine
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good target to tune total BIA output while preserving branch distribution

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Redirects flux toward laudanine/papaverine-related methylated products from reticuline or norreticuline
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Detected upon BBE suppression, indicating pathway redirection
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review discusses both reticuline 7-O-methyltransferase and norreticuline-specific N7OMT as branch alternatives.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 7OMT activity diverts reticuline-family intermediates toward alternative BIA products. 2. Laudanine was detected in BBE-suppressed lines, consistent with flux redirection through 7OMT. 3. A norreticuline-specific N7OMT suggests papaverine may branch before N-methylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme isolation; biochemical characterization; transgenic pathway perturbation inference
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates a 7-hydroxyl group on reticuline-family alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Papaverine is a muscle relaxant; laudanine is a BIA derivative
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful branch-diversion tool for generating alternative BIAs

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates scoulerine-derived major BIA branch and strongly controls downstream alkaloid accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Initially ER-targeted and subsequently vacuolar; also ER-colocalized in opium poppy cell cultures
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression decreased certain amino acids; antisense suppression increased amino acid pools and eliminated downstream BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cell cultures; laticifers/vascular tissues in species-specific contexts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: RNAi and antisense produced different upstream metabolite outcomes, suggesting metabolic channels or context dependence.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Localized to sieve elements in opium poppy and ER-associated compartments in cultured cells
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Scoulerine-derived benzylisoquinoline alkaloids such as sanguinarine and berberine branch products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch control point/bottleneck supported by transgenic perturbation
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Major scoulerine branch of BIA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BBE converts (S)-reticuline to (S)-scoulerine, initiating a major BIA branch. 2. Overexpression increases downstream alkaloids, whereas antisense suppression abolishes them and alters primary amino acid pools. 3. Divergent RNAi/antisense outcomes support metabolic channeling or strong context dependence.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; biochemical characterization; overexpression; antisense suppression; RNAi; localization
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: FAD-linked oxidase/flavoprotein with bicovalent FAD attachment
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fungal elicitor in benzophenanthridine-producing cell cultures
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively converts (S)-reticuline to (S)-scoulerine by forming the berberine bridge
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Produces antimicrobial and pharmacologically active BIAs
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong leverage point for increasing or redirecting BIA flux, but perturbation can strongly affect primary metabolism and channeling

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels scoulerine toward protoberberine alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: First reported O-methyltransferase clone specifically implicated in BIA metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SOMT methylates scoulerine to form tetrahydrocolumbamine. 2. It was the first cloned BIA-specific O-methyltransferase. 3. It defines an alternative branch from scoulerine in some species.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; biochemical characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates scoulerine at the 9-O position
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Protoberberines such as berberine are antimicrobial and medicinal
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Branch-selection target for protoberberine alkaloid production

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes palmatine/berberine-related protoberberine branch metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Active only on protoberberine alkaloids such as scoulerine or tetrahydrocolumbamine.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CoOMT methylates protoberberine intermediates to yield palmatine-related products. 2. Recombinant enzyme showed narrow specificity for protoberberine substrates. 3. It helps define substrate specificity in the protoberberine branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant enzyme assay
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to protoberberine alkaloid substrates to form palmatine-related products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Protoberberines are antimicrobial and medicinal
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful specificity module for protoberberine tailoring

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Forms methylenedioxy bridge in berberine/canadine branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Parallel path via columbamine judged unlikely because enzyme does not accept columbamine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: High specificity for tetrahydrocolumbamine.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyp719A1/canadine synthase forms a methylenedioxy bridge in the protoberberine pathway. 2. It is highly specific for tetrahydrocolumbamine. 3. Its specificity constrains possible alternative routes to berberine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Forms a methylenedioxy bridge on tetrahydrocolumbamine to produce canadine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Berberine-related alkaloids are antimicrobial and medicinal
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Specific branch-finishing enzyme for berberine/canadine engineering

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies quaternary ammonium intermediate for sanguinarine branch
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of few plant enzymes that forms quaternary ammonium compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TNMT methylates stylopine to cis-N-methylstylopine. 2. It shows narrow substrate specificity for tetrahydroprotoberberine alkaloids. 3. This step contributes to benzophenanthridine alkaloid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology-based cloning; functional characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to stylopine nitrogen to form cis-N-methylstylopine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sanguinarine is antimicrobial
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Specialized target for sanguinarine-branch engineering

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Commits reticuline-derived flux into the morphinan alkaloid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Laticifers accumulate morphinans though enzyme localizes outside latex
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified based on higher expression in morphine-containing Papaver species.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher expression in morphine-containing Papaver species; absent from isolated latex
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SalSyn catalyzes intramolecular phenol coupling of reticuline to salutaridine. 2. The gene was cloned by targeting transcripts elevated in morphine-producing Papaver species. 3. It defines entry into the morphinan branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative expression-based cloning; functional characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively couples (R)-reticuline to form salutaridine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Morphine and codeine are major analgesic/cough suppressant pharmaceuticals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key branch-commitment target for morphinan engineering

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces 7(S)-salutaridinol required for thebaine formation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suppression of downstream SalAT caused salutaridine accumulation, suggesting possible enzyme complex/channel
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SDR family enzyme with unusual monomeric higher molecular weight.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SalR stereospecifically reduces salutaridine to 7(S)-salutaridinol. 2. This stereospecificity is essential for the next step toward thebaine. 3. Evidence from SalAT silencing suggests functional coupling between SalR and SalAT.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; recombinant enzyme assay; homology modeling
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the keto group of salutaridine to form 7(S)-salutaridinol
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Morphinans are major pharmaceuticals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important for stereochemically correct morphinan synthesis; likely best deployed with SalAT

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acetylates salutaridinol to enable spontaneous thebaine formation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNAi silencing caused unexpected salutaridine accumulation, supporting channeling/complex effects
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BAHD family acetyltransferase; evidence supports SalR-SalAT enzyme complex.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein in sieve elements; transcript in companion cells
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; but central channeling node
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SalAT specifically acetylates 7(S)-salutaridinol. 2. The product spontaneously rearranges to thebaine. 3. RNAi lines accumulated salutaridine instead of salutaridinol, implying metabolic channeling with SalR.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; biochemical characterization; RNAi silencing; localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates salutaridinol at the 7-O position, enabling spontaneous conversion to thebaine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Thebaine is a morphinan intermediate used for opioid alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical for morphinan branch completion; maintain coupled function with SalR during engineering

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases morphine and codeine accumulation by reducing codeinone
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNAi silencing caused far-upstream (S)-reticuline accumulation rather than codeinone buildup, suggesting feedback or channel disruption
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression increased morphine, codeine, and unexpectedly thebaine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein in sieve elements; transcript in companion cells
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Penultimate step; functional control point supported by transgenic modulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COR1 reduces codeinone in the penultimate stage of morphine biosynthesis. 2. Overexpression increased morphine and codeine content. 3. Silencing caused anomalous reticuline accumulation, supporting feedback regulation or metabolic channeling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification; cloning; overexpression; RNAi silencing
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces codeinone to codeine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Morphine is an analgesic; codeine is a cough suppressant/analgesic
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful lever for increasing morphinan output, but may perturb upstream pathway organization

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates nicotine and tropane alkaloid biosynthesis by producing N-methylputrescine
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Single-gene overexpression gives only modest alkaloid increases, but synergizes strongly with H6H coexpression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots for biosynthesis; nicotine accumulates in aerial tissues after transport
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of first successful gene discoveries integrating metabolite and expression profiling; localized to endodermis/outer cortex/xylem in Nicotiana sylvestris, and pericycle in tropane plants.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root endodermis, outer cortex, and xylem in N. sylvestris; pericycle in Atropa/Hyoscyamus roots
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Integrated metabolite and gene expression profiling used for discovery
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Early committed step; modest alone but synergistic with H6H
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PMT catalyzes the first dedicated methylation step in nicotine and tropane alkaloid biosynthesis. 2. It was cloned using combined metabolite and expression profiling. 3. Coexpression with H6H produces synergistic increases in tropane alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; homology analysis; transgenic coexpression; tissue localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to putrescine to form N-methylputrescine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nicotine defends against herbivores; tropane alkaloids are anticholinergic pharmaceuticals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong early-step target, especially for combinatorial engineering with late steps such as H6H

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces 4-methylaminobutanal leading to N-methyl-Δ1-pyrrolium cation for nicotine/tropane biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Copper amine oxidase; cDNA isolated by homology-based cloning exploiting copper cofactor class.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MPO oxidatively deaminates N-methylputrescine. 2. The product spontaneously cyclizes to the central N-methyl-Δ1-pyrrolium cation. 3. MPO was cloned by a homology strategy based on its copper amine oxidase class.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology-based cloning; enzyme class assignment
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively deaminates N-methylputrescine to 4-methylaminobutanal
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nicotine defends against herbivores; tropane alkaloids are medicinal anticholinergics
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful precursor-supply target for nicotine/tropane pathway assembly

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts nicotine to undesirable nornicotine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified from differential transcript abundance in nicotine- vs nornicotine-accumulating varieties.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Differential among varieties; Cyp82E5v2 shows distinct tissue-specific expression
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Differential transcript profiling of P450 cDNAs
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyp82E4 encodes nicotine N-demethylase. 2. It converts nicotine to nornicotine, an undesirable precursor of a carcinogenic nitrosamine. 3. Differential expression among cultivars enabled its identification.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Differential transcript analysis; cloning; enzyme activity assignment
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively demethylates nicotine to form nornicotine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nornicotine is undesirable because it leads to carcinogenic N'-nitrosonornicotine
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major editing target for low-nornicotine tobacco improvement

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces tropinone to tropine, directing flux to hyoscyamine/scopolamine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots for biosynthesis; scopolamine accumulates after transport
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutagenesis/domain swapping identified residues controlling stereospecificity relative to TR-II.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TR-I stereospecifically reduces tropinone to tropine (3α). 2. This reaction directs metabolism toward hyoscyamine and scopolamine. 3. Structural studies revealed determinants of opposite stereospecificity versus TR-II.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; enzyme assay; X-ray crystallography; mutagenesis; localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces tropinone to tropine with 3α stereochemistry
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Scopolamine and hyoscyamine are anticholinergic pharmaceuticals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key branch-selector for favoring medicinal tropane alkaloids over calystegines

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces tropinone to pseudotropine, directing flux to calystegines
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Companion cells of sieve elements in phloem
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Localization in potato phloem companion cells indicates cell-type specialization for calystegine pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Companion cells of sieve elements in phloem
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Calystegine branch of tropane alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TR-II reduces tropinone to pseudotropine with opposite stereochemistry to TR-I. 2. This directs metabolism toward calystegines rather than medicinal tropanes. 3. Tissue localization in potato indicates phloem specialization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; enzyme assay; structural analysis; localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces tropinone to pseudotropine with 3β stereochemistry
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Calystegines are glucosidase inhibitors and may serve ecological roles
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for suppressing calystegine branch when prioritizing medicinal tropanes

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes littorine rearrangement toward hyoscyamine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified by virus-induced gene silencing-based functional genomics.
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Hyoscyamine/scopolamine branch of tropane alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cyp80F1 oxidizes and rearranges littorine to hyoscyamine aldehyde. 2. It was isolated using VIGS-based functional genomics. 3. This resolves a key rearrangement step in tropane alkaloid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Virus-induced gene silencing; cloning; functional characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes and rearranges (R)-littorine to hyoscyamine aldehyde
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Hyoscyamine/scopolamine are medicinal anticholinergics
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical resolved step for reconstructing high-value tropane alkaloid pathways

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts hyoscyamine to scopolamine and acts as a rate-limiting step in scopolamine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Modest increase alone, but strong synergistic increase with PMT coexpression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Scopolamine-accumulating tissues after transport from roots
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Constitutive transgene increased scopolamine in Atropa belladonna and shifted hyoscyamine toward scopolamine in A. baetica.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Pericycle of roots in Atropa belladonna and Hyoscyamus muticus
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. H6H catalyzes the final two-step conversion of hyoscyamine to scopolamine. 2. Transgenic overexpression increases scopolamine accumulation and shifts pathway flux toward scopolamine. 3. Coexpression with PMT shows synergistic enhancement of tropane alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; transgenic overexpression; tissue localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates hyoscyamine at 6β and promotes epoxide formation to produce scopolamine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Scopolamine is an important anticholinergic pharmaceutical
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent target for boosting scopolamine, especially together with PMT

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates caffeine/theobromine biosynthesis by 7-N-methylating xanthosine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Structural studies suggest possible coupled methylation and nucleoside cleavage by a single enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Seed developmental stages studied in Coffea canephora EST project
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: ESTs from Coffea canephora seed development; cDNAs from C. arabica
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. XMT/XRS catalyzes the first committed methylation in caffeine biosynthesis. 2. cDNAs were isolated from coffee. 3. Structural work suggested a coupled reaction mechanism involving nucleoside cleavage.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; structural analysis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to xanthosine at N7 to form 7-methylxanthosine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Caffeine/theobromine act as stimulants and may contribute to plant defense
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core target for caffeine manipulation in coffee and heterologous caffeine engineering

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Performs dual methylations to produce caffeine from 7-methylxanthine-derived intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Related enzymes in tea/coffee/cacao show single- or dual-methylation specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Caffeine synthase catalyzes two sequential N-methylations leading to caffeine. 2. Related homologs with single-methylation specificity exist in coffee, tea, and cacao. 3. These enzymes are highly homologous and evolved within a methyltransferase family related to carboxyl-methyltransferases.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; substrate specificity characterization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates 7-methylxanthine to theobromine and then methylates theobromine to caffeine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Caffeine is a stimulant and can enhance pest resistance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential for caffeine engineering in heterologous plants and likely target for caffeine reduction strategies

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls theobromine formation and strongly influences final caffeine content
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNA suppression also reduced XMT and DXMT transcripts due to sequence homology
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Suppression reduced theobromine and caffeine by 50–70% in C. arabica and C. canephora.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as influential control step for caffeine content
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MXMT catalyzes the N3 methylation producing theobromine. 2. Suppression of MXMT significantly lowered theobromine and caffeine in coffee. 3. Off-target co-suppression of XMT and DXMT occurred because of high homology.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene suppression in C. arabica and C. canephora; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates 7-methylxanthine at N3 to produce theobromine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Caffeine/theobromine are stimulants; caffeine can deter pests
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major practical target for low-caffeine coffee, though homology-driven off-target effects must be managed

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes final N1 methylation to caffeine
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Homology with MXMT and XMT can cause co-suppression in coffee RNAi strategies
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves in transgenic tobacco; seeds in coffee
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression with XMT and MXMT in transgenic tobacco accumulated ~5 μg g−1 FW caffeine in leaves and reduced pest damage by ~50%.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpression of all three N-methyltransferases in transgenic Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXMT catalyzes the final methylation at N1 in caffeine biosynthesis. 2. Together with XMT and MXMT, its expression enables caffeine production in non-caffeine plants. 3. Transgenic caffeine accumulation improved pest resistance in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; transgenic overexpression in tobacco; metabolite and pest assays
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to dimethylxanthine at N1 to form caffeine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Caffeine acts as stimulant and pest deterrent
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for caffeine biofortification of pest-resistant crops or final-step editing for low-caffeine coffee

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates pyrrolizidine alkaloid biosynthesis by forming homospermidine
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Root endodermis and cortex adjacent to phloem in Senecio vernalis; throughout root cortex in Eupatorium cannabinum
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Inflorescences/flowers for many Senecio alkaloids after transport from roots
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Recruited evolutionarily from deoxyhypusine synthase; multiple independent recruitment events inferred.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Actively growing roots; cell-type patterns differ by lineage
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenetic analysis of HSS and DHS homologs
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HSS catalyzes the first committed step in pyrrolizidine alkaloid biosynthesis. 2. It evolved by recruitment from deoxyhypusine synthase. 3. Its root localization supports systemic transport of pyrrolizidine alkaloids to shoots and inflorescences.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Molecular cloning; phylogenetic analysis; tissue localization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an aminobutyl moiety to putrescine to form homospermidine from spermidine and putrescine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against herbivores and ecological interactions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primary target for controlling pyrrolizidine alkaloid production and toxicity

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising regulatory lever to coordinately upregulate BIA biosynthetic genes in berberine-producing systems
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A WRKY transcription factor from Coptis japonica was identified by coordinated expression with BIA biosynthetic genes. 2. RNAi and overexpression showed that it specifically regulates BIA biosynthetic gene expression. 3. It represents the first transcription factor putatively involved in BIA metabolism identified by genomics approach.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cell culture context in berberine-producing lines
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific gene symbol/name not provided in review
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: DNA macroarray-based discovery; RNAi; overexpression analysis
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Coordinate expression with BIA pathway status; specific upstream signal not defined
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Coordinate expression with BIA biosynthetic genes in berberine-producing cell cultures
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes expression of benzylisoquinoline alkaloid biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: First genomics-derived regulator specifically linked to BIA metabolism in this review

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful activator for partial MIA pathway induction, but should be combined with precursor supply and additional regulators/repressor silencing
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORCA3 is an octadecanoid-responsive AP2-domain regulator isolated by activation tagging in C. roseus. 2. It activates AS, TDC, STR, D4H, cytochrome P450 reductase, and DXS, but not G10H, SGD, or DAT. 3. ORCA3 overexpression increased alkaloid accumulation only when exogenous loganin was supplied, indicating incomplete pathway control.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Octadecanoid derivative response; exogenous loganin required for alkaloid increase
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Represents partial but not global control over MIA biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA activation tagging; constitutive overexpression; target gene expression analysis; metabolite response to loganin
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Fails to activate all pathway genes; may require combinatorial regulatory engineering
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates AS; TDC; STR; D4H; cytochrome P450 reductase; DXS; does not activate G10H; SGD; DAT
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partial activation of MIA pathway; insufficient alone for full alkaloid enhancement
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of MIA flux via activation of multiple biosynthetic genes, but not full pathway
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: AS; TDC; STR; D4H; cytochrome P450 reductase; DXS

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for stacking with ORCA3 and repressor silencing to activate more complete MIA biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORCA2 is a transcriptional activator implicated in MIA pathway regulation. 2. The review suggests combined overexpression of ORCA2 and ORCA3 may be needed for full pathway activation. 3. It is discussed as part of combinatorial regulatory engineering rather than as a singly resolved master regulator.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Details less extensive than for ORCA3 in this review
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Inference from transgenic/regulatory studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely insufficient alone; requires combinatorial manipulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports activation of MIA biosynthetic capacity
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributory activator rather than fully decisive alone

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Silencing may relieve repression and should be considered with ORCA activators for pathway-wide enhancement
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. G-box binding factor 1 is described as a repressor of MIA pathway activation. 2. The review suggests silencing GBF1 together with overexpressing ORCA activators may be required for full pathway induction. 3. It is part of the repressive layer limiting complete MIA pathway expression.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific direct targets not detailed in the review text provided
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Repression may constrain full pathway engineering
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses components of the MIA pathway
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a negative regulator that must be countered for stronger MIA production

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Silencing may aid full MIA pathway activation in combination engineering strategies
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GBF2 is cited as a repressor of MIA biosynthesis. 2. The review proposes silencing GBF2 along with activator overexpression to improve pathway induction. 3. It is part of the regulatory brake on alkaloid accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific direct targets not given in provided text
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Repressive effect may limit engineering outcomes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses components of the MIA pathway
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Negative regulator to remove in pathway engineering

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Silencing repressor(s) may complement ORCA activators for full MIA pathway activation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Zinc finger protein family repressors are mentioned as negative regulators of MIA biosynthesis. 2. The review suggests their silencing may be necessary together with ORCA activator overexpression. 3. They represent an unresolved repressive layer in pathway regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Candidate negative regulator family constraining pathway-wide induction
